title: Is the Human Instrumentality Project like Rokos Basilisk
display_title: Is the Human Instrumentality Project like Rokos Basilisk
wikitext:
[]: Is the Human Instrumentality Project like Roko's Basilisk??             Quote Post by VeryConfusedDingus » Tue Jun 11, 2024 5:15 pm Hiya, newbie here. I recently finished the Evangelion show and EoE film, and yes I am emotionally traumatised thanks for asking. Probably my first introduction to the 'lore' behind this messed-up franchise before I had seen Eva was this video of Alex Jones inadvertently explaining the story (unsure if it's accurate but I thought it was funny): SPOILER: Show

[]: Re: Is the Human Instrumentality Project like Roko's Basilisk??             Quote Post by Axx°N N. » Tue Jun 11, 2024 6:02 pm Ha, I've seen a similar Alex Jones video where he inadvertently describes the goings-on in Twin Peaks . Which, if you can make it past a seriously uneven second season, you get to a film that takes it up to 11 similar to EoE, and a third season that has to be one of the most astonishingly avant-garde stretches of TV ever made. Anyway, as for Roko's Basilisk, I'm not sure I see the connection? Can you elaborate? I'm a fan of the concept and have always found it intriguing in a creepypasta fashion ... but it seems to me to be conceptual. I don't really think of Eva as being conceptual, but more about character drama. Instrumentality's place in the narrative is servicing the human stories at play, because it serves to juxtapose and emphasize the core theme of feeling disconnected from others and how painful and sometimes impossible it is to bridge that gap. Instrumentality serves as a challenge or even temptation to the beliefs of the characters. Instrumentality isn't a self-aware or automatic process--it's fully initiated by humans, and steered by consciousness. It's worth pointing out that it arises as a result of tampering with non-human physics and processes, but I don't think it can be compared to technological processes like AI. I guess I'm coming from a place of familiarity with the "agreed upon" meanings. In my head it's very firmly established that Rei houses Lilith's soul, for instance. If you want to read up on the popular readings on the series, these pages are good starts: https://wiki.evageeks.org/Category:Theory_and_Analysis

[]: Re: Is the Human Instrumentality Project like Roko's Basilisk??             Quote Post by VeryConfusedDingus » Wed Jun 12, 2024 1:51 am I have heard about Twin Peaks before, I saw it in a recommendations list after I finished The X-Files. I might watch it tonight. Every scene in EoE left me on the edge of my seat and I felt like Charlie in that scene of It's Always Sunny in Philadelphia trying to connect the dots. Feels like a breath of fresh air considering how tame and watered-down a lot of modern media is. I don't think an anime like this could have been made today without controversy. The reason I brought up Roko's Basilisk is not so much the idea that it is AI, but that we inadvertently become slaves to our twisted sense of progress and end up even more miserable. We became so invested in being "better" that we became stripped of our identity entirely and now exist in this singular hellish blob of flesh. Probably could have used a better example to articulate this. That's the image I got from seeing the third impact for the first time. Like some sort of rapture end-times event? The crosses that emerge in the atmosphere reminded me of that. I noted that the Kaballah was included in numerous scenes throughout the episodes, I'm not too bright so I will have to do some further reading to wrap my head around this. Shinji says LCL tastes like blood, and (from what I know) blood is the most primordial and basic component of the soul in Kabbalism. The lyrics "It all returns to nothing" reminded me of a gnostic worldview of needing to "fix the world" by merging it all into one singular collective. Neither good or nor evil, but whole. My interpretation is that Shinji, the tortured messiah-like figure, learns to love himself even in-spite of his flaws and rejects the HIP - thus leaving him in the ruins of a dead and fallen world, just like Adam and Eve during the Fall of Man SPOILER: Show

[]: Re: Is the Human Instrumentality Project like Roko's Basilisk??             Quote Post by Axx°N N. » Fri Jun 14, 2024 11:45 pm Incidentally I'm currently watching the X-Files. The creators were huge Twin Peaks fans, and there's lots of shared connections and aesthetics. VeryConfusedDingus wrote: Every scene in EoE left me on the edge of my seat and I felt like Charlie in that scene of It's Always Sunny in Philadelphia trying to connect the dots. Feels like a breath of fresh air considering how tame and watered-down a lot of modern media is.

[]: Re: Is the Human Instrumentality Project like Roko's Basilisk??             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Sat Jun 15, 2024 2:42 am In the jargon of 20 years ago, it is shown as a generally Friendly hard-takeoff upload Singularity in NGE TV, and as a not-so-Friendly SysOp variant in EoE. Related my thoughts on the subject from 2005 (so very dated tech references, and likely dead links) - Neon Genesis Eschaton .

[]: Re: Is the Human Instrumentality Project like Roko's Basilisk??             Quote Post by VeryConfusedDingus » Sat Jun 15, 2024 5:28 am Axx°N N. wrote: IIRC, the interview snippet about religious symbolism has been pulled from its specific context and used as a kind of thought-terminating cliche ... what was said was closer to "the religious symbolism doesn't have Christian meaning" as in, the creators don't consider it an authentically religious work of art in the same manner as an Orthodox icon. It doesn't mean there isn't an internal consistency to its presence in the narrative, and I'd say there's plenty of evidence the religious symbolism had thought put behind it to at least that extent. It's true that "rule of cool" is indeed a Gainax trademark, but I don't think it totally nulls the fact the symbolism is used in a very apt way.

[]: Re: Is the Human Instrumentality Project like Roko's Basilisk??             Quote Post by Axx°N N. » Wed Jun 19, 2024 5:51 am VeryConfusedDingus wrote: Is Kaworu meant to symbolize the ideal person Shinji wishes to be?

[]: Re: Is the Human Instrumentality Project like Roko's Basilisk??             Quote Post by VeryConfusedDingus » Wed Jun 19, 2024 4:21 pm Axx°N N.#943305 wrote: Yes! I'm impressed you arrived at this conclusion on your own, I for sure never did. Anno has said in an interview that this was the intention, and that Kaworu is an idealized form of a self-actualized Shinji. You can read more on said interview here . As a fan of Shinji and Kaworu, you might be interested to know this interview is mostly about that relationship, and was published in an outright Yaoi magazine. It was the longest interview Anno had given up to that point and is pretty significant.
